definition: 32-bit IEEE float (1 sign / 8 exponent / 23 mantissa), ~7 decimal digits. The accuracy baseline — often kept for 'master weights' and accumulation even when the math runs in lower precision.
NVIDIA Tensor Core format that keeps FP32's 8 exponent bits but truncates the mantissa (~10 bits). Gives near-FP32 range with much faster matrix math — a drop-in speedup for FP32 workloads.
16-bit float (1 / 5 / 10). Halves memory and doubles throughput vs FP32, but its small 5-bit exponent range risks overflow/underflow — typically used with 'loss scaling' during training.

16-bit float (1 / 8 / 7) that keeps FP32's full 8-bit exponent range but fewer mantissa bits. Same dynamic range as FP32 makes it far easier to train with than FP16 — now the default for much AI training.

8-bit float in two variants — E4M3 (more precision) and E5M2 (more range). Popularized by NVIDIA's Hopper Transformer Engine; roughly doubles throughput and halves memory vs FP16 for training and inference.
6-bit float, an intermediate step between FP8 and FP4. Supported on Blackwell and AMD CDNA 4, it trades a little accuracy for more inference throughput and smaller memory footprint.

4-bit float (e.g., E2M1) — the lowest-precision format in current AI hardware. Blackwell and AMD MI350 use it to maximize inference tokens/sec; needs careful per-block scaling (MX formats) to hold accuracy.

8-bit integer format widely used for inference quantization. Very efficient, but requires calibration (mapping float ranges to integers) to avoid accuracy loss; common in edge and cost-sensitive serving.

Running most math in a low-precision format (FP16/BF16/FP8) for speed while keeping select values — master weights and accumulations — in higher precision to preserve accuracy. Standard practice in modern training.

Reducing the numeric precision of model weights/activations (e.g., to FP8, INT8, or FP4) to cut memory and boost speed. The goal is maximum compression with minimal accuracy loss — central to cheap inference.
An OCP standard (MXFP8/MXFP6/MXFP4) where a block of low-precision values shares a common scaling factor. This shared scale is what makes FP4/FP6 usable at scale; adopted across NVIDIA and AMD.
NVIDIA tech (Hopper onward) that dynamically chooses per-layer precision — e.g., FP8 vs FP16 — and manages scaling automatically, so models get low-precision speed without manual tuning.
